head.ce:title: Growth of CQD/PPy on cotton cloth as a binder-free high-performance flexible electrode for symmetric supercapacitor

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, benefited from the continuous development of nanomaterials [1–3], wearable micro-electronic technology with the characteristics of flexibility and miniaturization has flourished [4,5]. Flexible electronics demonstrate a wide range of applications that are difficult to achieve with traditional rigid electronics, such as robot sensing skin, smart clothing for health monitoring, flexible deformable displays, etc. [6–10]. For wearable applications, micropower supplies with high energy density, flexibility and long service life are essential [11,12]. In addition, considering the direct contact between micropower and the human body in daily use, safety is also a necessary feature of micropower [13]. As one of the most important flexible electronic devices, flexible supercapacitors have attracted much attention because of their good flexibility, good safety, and long cycle life [14,15]. To meet the demands of flexible supercapacitors, properties such as thinness, transparency, flexibility, stretchability, insulation and corrosion resistance have become the key indicators of flexible substrates [16–18]. Compared to soft metals [19], organic materials[20] and hydrogels[21–23], textile materials such as cotton and fibers are valued for their low cost, high strength, and flexibility [24–26].

The discovery by Heeger et al. in 1977 changed the perception that polymers could only be used as insulating materials [27]. In recent years, conductive polymers such as polyaniline (PANI) [28,29], polypyrrole (PPy) [30,31] and polythiophene (PTh) [32] have been widely studied in supercapacitors due to their high capacitance and low cost characteristics. PPy also benefits from a unique π-conjugated system that provides an efficient path for electron charge flow [33]. In addition, PPy exhibits excellent environmental stability in both oxidized (p-doped) and redox (n-doped) forms [34,35] and can be used in a variety of smart applications such as supercapacitors and sensors [36,37]. The combination of conductive polymers and flexible substrates creates new frontiers for wearable electronics, and currently the common methods used are printing and weaving. Cho et al. early proposed a simple strategy for patterning conductive polymers using inkjet printing with vapor phase polymerization [38]. However, the plate thickness during printing directly affects the conductivity of the printed conductive polymer, and precise control of the printing procedure is challenging. Another approach is to bond a conductive polymer to a flexible substrate by means of a textile technique. Lund et al. provided a detailed discussion of conductive fibers for use in e-textiles [25]. Conductive yarns are inserted into textile fabrics by weaving, knitting or braiding mechanisms. However, the pursuit of high electrical conductivity precludes the use of stiffer fibers, which results in insufficient mechanical properties for the subsequent application life of conductive textiles.

Cotton cloth (CC) was purchased from Changzhou Buku Textile Co. Ltd. (Jiangsu, China). Formic acid (HCOOH), stannous chloride dihydrate (SnCl2·2H2O), hydrochloric acid (HCl), pyrrole (Py), citric acid (CA), urea, sodium p-toluenesulfonate (C7H7NaO3S) and iron (III) chloride hexahydrate (FeCl3·6H2O) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). All chemical reagents are of analytical grade and can be used without further purification. Double distilled ultrapure water (DI water) with a resistance greater than 18.3 MΩ⋅cm was used for the preparation of all solutions.

The CC was cut to a suitable size (1 × 1 cm), cleaned repeatedly by ultrasonic with DI water and ethanol, and then dried in an air blast drying oven. The soaking solution was prepared as follows: 50 mL of DI water mixed with 50 mL HCOOH and then the 0.5 g SnCl2·2H2O and 0.8 mL HCl was slowly added with vigorous stirring until the mixture solution was clear. At last, the clean CC was soaked for 2 h in soaking solution, and then rinsed in DI water and dried.

0.1 g CA and 0.1 g urea were dissolved into 10 mL of DI water. The above solution and the treated CC were transferred into a 25 mL Teflon lined autoclave and kept at 180 °C for 5 h to deposit CQD nanosheets on CC. After hydrothermal treatment, the CC@CQD composite electrode was rinsed several times with DI water to remove unreacted chemicals and vacuum dried.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of CC@CQD@PPy and CC@PPy composite electrodes
ce:section.ce:para: The CC@CQD composite electrode was put into 40 mL 0.1 M C7H7NaO3S solution containing 0.1 M Py. Then, a FeCl3 solution was slowly dripped under mechanical agitation to start the polymerization. The concentration of FeCl3 solution ranged from 0.1 to 0.5 M, and then reacted in an ice water bath (0–4 °C) for 24 h. After reaction, the CC@CQD@PPy composite electrode was obtained after rinsing with DI water. The CC@PPy composite electrode was obtained by replacing the original CC@CQD composite electrode with CC.

To facilitate the comparison of the influence of CQD on electrochemical performance, the CC@CQD@PPy composite electrodes were prepared for different mass of CA and urea (0.1–0.5 g), named as CC@CQD-x@PPy (x=1, 2, 3, 4 and 5).

The morphology and structure of the as-prepared samples were characterized by field emission scanning electron microscope (FESEM, Hitachi S-4800) and transmission scanning microscopy (TEM, LGJ-10 N). The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, ESCALAB Xi+) and the Diffraction of x-rays (XRD, Rigaku D/max-2200PC) were used to analyze the surface elements composition and the crystal structure of the prepared composites. Raman spectrum was taken with laser confocal micro-Raman spectroscopy (Horiba LabRAM HR Evolution). The molecular structure was identified by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR, Thermo Scientific Nicolet iS20). Fluorescence spectrometry and UV-Vis absorption spectra were carried out on a PerkinElmer LS55 fluorescence photometer and Shi-madzu UV-2450.

The electrodes were cut into 1×1 cm and the densities of all electrodes were averaged at 2.0–2.2 mg/cm2. Two electrodes with the same mass were selected to construct a symmetric supercapacitor, and the electrochemical characterization of the composites was carried out with two electrode system in 1.0 M Na2SO4 electrolyte. All electrochemical evaluations were completed in an electrochemical workstation system (CHI660E, Shanghai Chenhua Co., Ltd., China) and in a test environment of room temperature and standard atmospheric pressure.

The schematic of CC@CQD@PPy electrode preparation and application is shown in Fig. 1. Firstly, the CC was cut into 1×1 cm, and CQD grew directly on CC surface by hydrothermal reaction, followed by in-situ polymerization of pyrrole under mechanical stirring, and finally CC@CQD@PPy electrode was successfully prepared. CC@CQD@PPy electrode has excellent specific capacitance, cycling stability, and flexibility, and can be assembled with hydrogel to form an all-solid-state symmetric supercapacitor, which has great potential for application in the smart textile.

The surface morphology of CC, CC@PPy and CC@CQD-3@PPy composites was compared by field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM). Fig. 2A–C show photographs and FESEM images of the pure CC, which exhibit a white surface smoothness, and the diameter of the fibers in CC is about 11 μm. Photographs and FESEM images of the CC@PPy composite are shown in Fig. 2D–F. Pure PPy has serious self-stacking phenomenon (Fig. S1). However, in CC@PPy, after the in-situ polymerization of pyrrole, the cloth sheet changes from white to black, and the PPy was uniformly attached to CC. A dense PPy layer was formed on the surface of each fiber, showing a typical core-shell structure, but the PPy shell is looser [42]. CC@CQD@PPy also shows a black cloth sheet at the macroscopic level and still maintains the flexibility and overall structure of the fabric (Fig. 2G). As seen by the FESEM images (Fig. 2H-I), the fiber diameter of CC@CQD-3@PPy is 29 μm, which is approximately twice the fiber diameter of CC@PPy, and the fiber surface is more compact. This is attributed to that in the CC@CQD-3@PPy structure, CQD, as a zero-dimensional carbon-based material, can provide more active sites for the adhesion of PPy, thus allowing CQD@PPy to grow more firmly on CC fibers [43]. Electrical interactions between PPy and CQD form a continuous conduction pathway that enhances charge transfer [44]. It is worth mentioning that the interaction between CQD@PPy and CC is direct without binder, which reduces the electron transfer resistance and enhances the electron/ion transport in the composites[45]. The extremely small particle size of CQD resulted in CC@CQD not being able to be identified under FESEM, so the upper liquid layer during the preparation of CC@CQD was characterized. As shown in Fig. S2, small carbon nanoparticles with sizes between 1.5 and 6 nm can be observed by TEM. It is well known that photoluminescent is a remarkable property of CQD [46]. In order to characterize the key role of zero-dimensional CQD in the growth of PPy, FESEM tests were performed on CC@CQD-x@PPy (x=1, 2, 4 and 5). The fibres of CC@CQD-1@PPy in Fig. 2J shows a large bare area, which is caused by the weak binding of PPy, and a large amount of PPy would be dislodged during rinsing. The fibres of CC@CQD-2@PPy shows an increase in PPy loading, but there are still some bare areas (Fig. 2K). Compared to the uniform and tight growth of PPy on CC@CQD-3@PPy fibres (Fig. 2H), individually aggregated PPy appears between the fibres of both CC@CQD-4@PPy (Fig. 2L) and CC@CQD-5@PPy (Fig. 2M), which can lead to the problem of unstable structure of the electrode materials during charging and discharging. It can be seen that CQD provides growth sites for PPy attachment on CC fibres and also enhances the bonding strength of PPy, and CC@CQD-3@PPy is the most outstanding in terms of morphology. The moist CC, CC@CQD, and CC@CQD@PPy were placed under the UV light at the same time. It is clearly seen that CC@CQD shows fluorescence while CC and CC@CQD@PPy do not (Fig. 2N), indicating that the deposited PPy covers the fluorescence of CQD. In addition, CC@CQD and CC@CQD@PPy were immersed in water, respectively. Under the UV light, the cuvette immersed in CC@CQD shows strong fluorescence, while CC@CQD@PPy shows no phenomenon (Fig. 2O). This can be explained by the fact that the production of PPy immobilizes CQD well without being dispersed into the water, further indicating a strong interaction between PPy and CQD.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) can measure changes in the composition and chemical state of composite electrodes. Fig. 3A shows the full XPS spectrum of CC@CQD@PPy. The presence of C, N, O, Cl, and S can be clearly observed, in agreement with the results of energy-dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) as shown in Fig. S3. The peaks of C 1s and N 1s are at about 284 and 400 eV respectively. Based on the experimental C 1s curve (black line in Fig. 3B), four peaks were fitted at 284.3, 285.1, 286.3 and 288.1 eV, which are related to C-C bond, C-N bond, C-O bond, and CO bond, respectively [47]. Based on the black line in Fig. 3C, N 1s fitted peaks at 399.6 eV and 401.2 eV associated with N-H nitrogen atoms and -N+- cations in the PPy backbone, respectively [4]. The -N+- is crucial for the electrical conductivity of PPy because the direct electrostatic interactions between positive charges and electrons promote conduction [48]. X-ray diffraction (XRD) is utilized to analyze the crystal nature of composites. As can be seen in Fig. 3D, pure CC shows diffraction peaks at 2θ = 14.8°, 16.6°, 22.8°, 26.5°, and 34.2° corresponding to the (110), (002), (120), (103), and (123) planes, respectively, which match the standard JCPDS No. 50-0926, and no other impurity peaks were detected [49]. These diffractions represent the crystalline nature of CC due to cellulose crystals [50]. Due to the small amount of CQD, the peaks in the CC@CQD spectrum have no significant change. However, in CC@PPy, the intensities of all these diffraction peaks were weakened due to the coverage of CC by PPy, and the characteristic diffraction peaks of PPy were exhibited at 2θ = 22.2° (Fig. S4), showing the amorphous structure and short-range arrangement of PPy chains [51,52]. In addition, the intensity of the diffraction peak is further weakened in CC@CQD@PPy, which can also indicate that the introduction of CQD increases the amount of PPy attachment to the CC fibers. To further investigate the chemical composition of the substances on the CC surface and to eliminate the influence of CC on the experiments, the adherent substances on CC@PPy and CC@CQD@PPy were scraped and examined by Raman and Fourier transform infrared (FTIR). For the Raman spectra (Fig. 3E), the strong band observed at 1568 cm−1 matches the CC stretching vibration in PPy, while the C-N stretching vibration is assigned at 1358 cm−1 [53] The G-band (in-plane vibration of sp2 bonded atoms) and D-band (out of plane vibration) of the CQD are distributed at 1576 and 1353 cm−1 [54], where both G-band and D-band are Raman characteristic peaks of C crystals [55,56]. Upon compounding pyrrole on CC with CQD, the two peaks of the CQD@PPy composite are shifted compared to PPy and CQD, with peaks at 1554 and 1343 cm−1, which may be attributed to the
π − π
interaction and hydrogen bonding between CQD and PPy [57,58]. Fig. 3F shows the FTIR spectra of pure PPy, CQD and CQD@PPy composites. The FTIR spectra of CQD@PPy are mostly characterized by the characteristic peaks of PPy because of the low content of CQD compared to PPy. For example, the peak at 3374 cm−1 (broad peak) corresponds to the N-H stretching vibration in the pyrrole ring [59], and the presence of the vibrational bands at 2988 and 2881 cm−1 can be attributed to the vibration of aliphatic -CH3 and -CH2 groups in the PPy [60,61]. In addition, N-H and C-H in-plane deformation vibrations and C-H bending vibrations of the pyrrole ring also appeared at 1089, 1046 and 883 cm−1 [62–64].The peaks at 1377 and 1458 cm−1 are attributed to the N-H and CC bonding [65,66], which are shown in the spectra of both PPy and CQD. A weaker CO stretching vibrational peak of CQD appears at 1639 cm−1, which may correspond to the carbonyl group of an amide, ester, carboxylic acid, or ketone [67], suggesting the successful complexation of CQD with PPy.

The electrochemical performance of different composite electrodes was investigated by a two-electrode system in 1.0 M Na2SO4 electrolyte. Fig. 4A shows the CV curves of the three electrodes at 20 mV/s. It is obvious that the CV curves of the CC@CQD@PPy electrode show mirror symmetry and quasi-rectangular shape between the 0–1.0 V and have a larger area capacitance than that of the CC@PPy electrode, whereas the CV curves of the pure CC electrodes are negligible. This indicates that the significant enhancement of the capacitance of the electrode material originates from the active PPy, and the introduction of CQD also improves the electrochemical performance of the electrode in a large extent. The results of the GCD curves in Fig. 4B are consistent, where the CC@CQD@PPy electrode shows an approximately symmetric linear curve. CC@CQD@PPy electrode has a longer charge/discharge time and smaller voltage drop than the CC@PPy electrode, whereas the pure CC has no electrochemical properties at all. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) is an effective method to provide information between electrode and electrolyte. As shown in Fig. 4C, the effective internal resistance (
R
s
) of the electrochemical system is evaluated using the real-axis intercept of the high-frequency region, which is mainly derived from the solution resistance [68]. The
R
s
of the CC@PPy electrode is about 2.18 Ω, while the
R
s
of the CC@CQD@PPy electrode is 1.89 Ω. The charge-transfer resistance (
R
ct
) represents the charge diffusion resistance at the electrode/electrolyte interface and can be assessed from the semicircular diameter of the high frequency region in the inset of Fig. 4C. The
R
ct
of CC@CQD@PPy electrode is 1.89 Ω, which is much smaller than the CC@PPy electrode (2.18 Ω). In addition, the CC@CQD@PPy electrode has a small warburg resistance ( W ) in the low-frequency region, indicating fast ion diffusion/migration kinetics [69]. The excellent capacitance performance of the CC@CQD@PPy electrode can be attributed to the fact that the combination of the CQD and PPy effectively reduces the internal resistance of the ion/electron transfer during charging/discharging, which reduces the energy waste [44]. To further explore the role of CQD, the CC@CQD-x@PPy electrodes were investigated. The electrochemical performance of the composite electrodes increases and then decreases with the growth of CQD content, in which the CC@CQD-3@PPy electrode exhibited the largest CV area (Fig. 4D) and the longest charging and discharging time (Fig. 4E). The specific expressed per single electrode ( C ) calculated from the specific capacitance of devices used in two-electrode system (
C
spec − 2 E
) according to the GCD curves based on the Eq. (1) [70] and 2[71]:
(1)
C
spec − 2 E
=
I × ∆ t
2 m × ∆ V
(2)
C = 4
C
spec − 2 E
Where, I is the constant current (A),
∆ t
is the discharge time (s), m is mass of active material on the single electrode (g), and the symmetric supercapacitor is assembled by selecting the same mass of electrode material.
∆ V
is voltage (V). The results show that CC@CQD-1@PPy electrode has the lowest C of 300.1 F/g at 0.5 A/g, and the C of CC@CQD-2@PPy, CC@CQD-3@PPy and CC@CQD-4@PPy electrodes are 505.2, 537.9 and 511.2 F/g, while the C of CC@CQD-5@PPy electrode is reduced to 450.1 F/g. Fig. 4F shows the C of the composite electrodes versus discharge current density. The C of all CC@CQD-x@PPy electrodes show a decreasing trend with the increase of current density (0.5–2.5 A/g). From the above results, it can be concluded that an appropriate addition of the CQD content leads to an increase in the capacitance of the CC@CQD-x@PPy electrodes. A moderate concentration of CQD provides more active sites, increasing the amount of electrically active material attached to the CC, and improving the transport of electrons between PPy, and thus enhancing the electrochemical performance. In contrast, when the CQD is in excess, it leads to the aggregation of particles and breaks down the electron channels between PPy, reducing the electroactivity of the electrode materials[72]. The results of the fluorescence test of the solution of CC@CQD-x cloth sheet immersed in water are shown in Fig. S5, it can also be seen that the fluorescence intensity of CC@CQD-5 is the largest, which indicates that the CQD of CC@CQD-5 is in excess and unable to be immobilized on the CC fibers. Therefore, for the later study we chose the CC@CQD-3@PPy electrode material with better performance.

To clearly elucidate the role of CQD@PPy for capacitance enhancement and the optimization of CC as a flexible substrate for the structure, Fig. 5 shows a schematic diagram of PPy and CC@CQD@PPy electrodes. In PPy electrode (Fig. 5A), the exposed specific surface area of the active substance is greatly limited due to the agglomeration effect of PPy nanospheres, which causes difficulties for solvent ions to diffuse between the electrode material and the electrolyte, thus largely limiting the electrochemical performance [73–75]. In contrast, in the CC@CQD@PPy electrode (Fig. 5B), the fibers of CC show a longitudinal and transverse arrangement, and the introduction of CQD provides more active sites allowing PPy to grow uniformly on the fibers of CC. The electrolyte ions can easily diffuse into the fibers and fully contact with more active PPy, resulting in a great increase of the energy storage surface area. At the same time, the fibers are intertwined with each other, which provides a "highway" for electrons/ions and promotes the electron/ion transport. The strategy of preparing flexible textile electrodes by in-situ growth of PPy on CC under the promotion of CQD can effectively improve the energy storage performance and cycling stability of flexible supercapacitors, which also has great potential in the field of smart textiles.

Through the above study, CC@CQD-3@PPy electrode was selected to assemble a simple supercapacitor to further explore the electrochemical performance of CC@CQD@PPy supercapacitor in practical applications ( Fig. 6A). Fig. 6B shows the CV curves at different scan rates and the curves are nearly rectangular, showing the typical characteristic of double layer capacitance [76]. In addition, the shape of the CV curves is not greatly distorted even at higher scan rates, which indicates that the CC@CQD@PPy supercapacitor has fast charging and discharging characteristics. This is mainly due to the uniform growth of CQD and PPy nanostructures on the CC surface, which improves the ion/electron transport rate. Fig. 6C shows the GCD curves of CC@CQD@PPy supercapacitor at different current densities. The GCD curves are symmetric triangular, and the charging/discharging process has a high reversibility [77]. The energy density ( E ) and power density ( P ) are the two important factors that evaluate the performance of supercapacitors for practical application which are estimated by the following Eqs. (3) and (4) [78,79]:
(3)
E =
C
spec − 2 E
V
2
2 × 3600
(4)
P =
3600 E
∆ t
where, the corresponding
C
spec − 2 E
can be calculated from Eq. (1). After calculation, this asymmetric supercapacitor can provide a maximum E of 18.7 Wh/kg at a P of 125.0 W/kg. These results outperform some previously reported PPy-based flexible supercapacitors such as PPy/CNF (16.95 Wh/kg at 1.2 W/kg) [80], PPy:PSS/CNP (15 Wh/kg at 550 W/kg) [81], FCF/PPy (16.9 Wh/kg at 100 W/kg) [82], PPy@NiCo-LDH@SSY (6.6 Wh/kg at 159.2 W/kg)[83], PPy VANV/CNTF (6.22 Wh/kg at 123.25 W/kg) [84], SnO2@PPy/CC//SnO2@PPy/CC (0.7 Wh/kg at 4.74 W/kg) [85] and PVA-SA-PPy FSCs (0.0967 Wh/kg at 0.999 W/kg) [86].The Ragone plots of the CC@CQD@PPy supercapacitor are shown in Fig. 6D. In addition, the cycling stability of supercapacitor is also an important factor in evaluating the parameters required for their potential for practical applications. The CC@PPy and CC@CQD@PPy supercapacitors were subjected to 10,000 cycles at 20 A/g current density as shown in Fig. 6E. The capacitance retention rate of CC@PPy supercapacitor is 41.7 %, while CC@CQD@PPy supercapacitor can achieve a capacitance retention rate of 77.9 %, which can well demonstrate that the incorporation of CQD can provide the active sites for pyrrole polymerization at the same time enhance the adhesion of PPy on the CC fibers, thus conferring a good cycling stability to the CC@CQD@PPy supercapacitor. Fig. 6F shows the EIS data of the CC@CQD@PPy supercapacitor as well as the equivalent circuit diagram (inset of Fig. 6F), with a low
R
s
of 1.89 Ω. Additionally, a 45° sloped portion in the mid-frequency region suggests that the W of the CC@CQD@PPy supercapacitor is a result of the frequency dependence of the ion transport in the electrolyte and the electrochemical capacitive behavior of CC@CQD@PPy supercapacitor is not controlled by diffusion [87]. In summary, these excellent electrochemical properties indicate that the CC@CQD@PPy supercapacitor has excellent electrochemical performance and is substantially higher than many representative textile-based supercapacitors ( Table 1).

To demonstrate the flexibility and mechanical stability, the assembled CC@CQD@PPy supercapacitor was subjected to electrochemical tests in different bending states, where the schematic diagram is shown in Fig. 7A and the angle estimation method is shown in Fig. 7B. The electrochemical tests were performed utilizing two working electrode clips as collectors in 1 M Na2SO4 solution (Fig. 7C). Then the bending tests at 0°, 30°, 45°, 60° and 90° were performed sequentially (Fig. 7D). It is observed from the CV curves (Fig. 7E), GCD curves (Fig. 7F) and EIS curves (Fig. 7G) that the electrochemical properties of the CC@CQD@PPy supercapacitor are almost unchanged under different bending states, showing the excellent flexibility and higher mechanical stability, where in the EIS curves at 90° bending, the closer contact of the electrodes endows the supercapacitor a smaller resistance.

To investigate the practical application of CC@CQD@PPy electrode in the field of smart textiles, an all-solid-state symmetric supercapacitor was prepared by using MAA-Na2SO4 hydrogel as the electrolyte (the specific preparation steps are described in Method S1) and two CC@CQD@PPy electrodes as the positive and negative electrodes, and the schematic diagrams and photographs are shown in Fig. 8A–B. As the electrolyte changes from liquid to solid state, the motion of ions is drastically weakened, which leads to the deformation of the CV curve (Fig. 8C), and a large voltage drop in the GCD curve (Fig. 8D). The supercapacitor exhibits larger
R
s
(6.82 Ω) and
R
ct
(6.71 Ω) in the EIS spectra (Fig. 8E). Despite the large internal resistance of this all-solid-state supercapacitor, its E can still reach 7.8 Wh/kg at 124.5 W/kg, which has great potential in the field of smart textiles.

In summary, CC@CQD@PPy was prepared by using CC as a flexible substrate, uniformly growing a layer of CQD on CC, and then directly growing pyrrole directly on CC through in-situ polymerization under the initiation of Fe3+, realizing the close bonding of conductive polymers and flexible substrates. The obtained CC@CQD@PPy electrode has a high C of 537.9 F/g at 0.5 A/g. The fabricated CC@CQD@PPy symmetric supercapacitor exhibits an excellent E of 18.7 Wh/kg at P of 125.0 W/kg, and a supreme cycling stability with a capacitance retention of 77.9 % after 10,000 cycles. In addition, the CC@CQD@PPy supercapacitor has excellent flexibility, with almost unchanged electrochemical performance under different bending angles, and the all-solid-state supercapacitor has good electrochemical properties. This work confirms that the low-cost, sustainable CC@CQD@PPy flexible electrodes contribute significantly to the development of smart textiles in the future of wearable electronics.

Zhenglong Yang: Conceptualization. Wenlong Xu: Funding acquisition. Can Zhou: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Data curation. Guijing Liu: Supervision, Funding acquisition. Fengying Wang: Supervision. Huichen Liu: Data curation. Jihai Nai: Formal analysis. Junnan Hao: Supervision. Zhuyin Sui: Investigation.
